coredata.dc:title: Improving the electrochemical performance of nickel-cobalt organic framework by hybridizing with carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Guoping
Tang, Tian
Zhang, Lilei
Liao, Sen
coredata.dc:description: To improve the electrochemical properties of nickel-cobalt metal-organic skeleton materials, two carbon quantum dots (CQDs), namely, CQD-CA and N-doped CQD-CAn, were prepared by using citric acid as carbon source and then introduced into NiCo-MOF to prepare CQDs/NiCo-MOF composites. Through the application of physical characterization and electrochemical analysis methods, such as SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS and BET, it was found that NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn exhibited a nano thinner lamellar structure, higher specific surface area, larger pore structure, and excellent electron transport ability. Especially, by introducing CQDs, the charge could transfer from oxygen to Ni and Co, and there was some higher electron cloud density around active metals Ni and Co in NiCo-MOF@CAn than in NiCo-MOF, effectively activating the metal center Ni and Co. As the result, the specific capacitance of NiCo-MOF@CAn reached 1917.7 F·g−1 at the potential scan rate 5 mV·s−1, significantly higher than that of NiCo-MOF (1173.4 F·g−1) and NiCo-MOF@CA (1489.3 F·g−1). The method might provide an alternative strategy for modulating the morphology, porous structure and the partial electron structure of the target materials by introducing various CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Metal-organic framework
Carbon quantum dots
Composite material

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of NiCo-MOF@CA.
SEM images of (a) NiCo-MOF, (b) NiCo-MOF@CA, (c) NiCo-MOF@CAn.
TEM image and high-resolution TEM images of NiCo-MOF@CAn.
XRD patterns of NiCo-MOF, NiCo-MOF@CA, NiCo-MOF@CAn,
(a) FT-IR spectra of pristine NiCo-MOF, NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn, (b) XPS survey spectrum, high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) O 1s, (d) C 1s, (e) Ni 2p, (f) Co 2p.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm, (b) the corresponding BJH pore size distribution curves of the NiCo-MOF, NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn composites.
(a) CV curves at 5 mV s−1, (b) GCD curves at 1 A g−1 of all the MOF materials.
(a) CV curves of NiCo-MOF-CAn at 5–50 mV s−1, (b) the GCD curves of NiCo-MOF-CAn at 1–5 A·g−1.
(a) Nyquist plots of various NiCo-MOF with different CQD. (b) Cycle stability of various NiCo-MOF with different CQDs at 15 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Improving the electrochemical performance of nickel-cobalt organic framework by hybridizing with carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the electrochemical properties of nickel-cobalt metal-organic skeleton materials, two carbon quantum dots (CQDs), namely, CQD-CA and N-doped CQD-CAn, were prepared by using citric acid as carbon source and then introduced into NiCo-MOF to prepare CQDs/NiCo-MOF composites. Through the application of physical characterization and electrochemical analysis methods, such as SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS and BET, it was found that NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn exhibited a nano thinner lamellar structure, higher specific surface area, larger pore structure, and excellent electron transport ability. Especially, by introducing CQDs, the charge could transfer from oxygen to Ni and Co, and there was some higher electron cloud density around active metals Ni and Co in NiCo-MOF@CAn than in NiCo-MOF, effectively activating the metal center Ni and Co. As the result, the specific capacitance of NiCo-MOF@CAn reached 1917.7 F·g−1 at the potential scan rate 5 mV·s−1, significantly higher than that of NiCo-MOF (1173.4 F·g−1) and NiCo-MOF@CA (1489.3 F·g−1). The method might provide an alternative strategy for modulating the morphology, porous structure and the partial electron structure of the target materials by introducing various CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy plays an important role in the rapid development of economy. However, traditional energy reserves are faced with short supply, and the use of traditional energy often creates a large amount of greenhouse gas. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new energy for achieving sustainable and high-quality economic development. Supercapacitor, as a new type of energy storage device, has excellent pulse charging and discharging performance. Owing to its high power density and long cycle life, supercapacitor has shown great application potential in new energy, electric vehicles and consumer electronics [1–3].

Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials formed by covalent bonding between organic ligands and metal ions [4]. They display higher specific surface area [5]. Compared to other types of electrode materials, MOFs materials have more active sites, which are beneficial for full contacting with electrolyte solutions and promoting electrochemical reactions [6,7]. For example, Yang et al. [8] synthesized a new manganese-mixed imidazole salt molecular sieve framework-67, which showed a specific capacity of 926.25 F·g−1 at 0.5 A·g−1. Traditional porous coordination MOFs, such as ZIF series, has poor cycling performance, lower power density and lower specific capacity because of their poor conductivity. Therefore, the future research will focus on addressing MOFs' poor conductivity [9].

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are a new nanoscale carbon material with the common size ranging from a few nanometers to tens of nanometers [10]. Usually, CQDs are composed of carbon polymers or carbon nanoparticles. Their preparation methods can be divided into two categories, namely, top-down methods (such as arc discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical synthesis) and bottom-up methods (such as combustion, and hydrothermal). Of which, one-step hydrothermal method is prevailing. It applies citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials and utilizes water and ethanol as solvents, greatly reducing the difficulty of subsequent purification and environmental pollution [11,12]. They show excellent optoelectronic properties，chemical stability, larger specific surface area, edge defects and abundant surface functional groups, which have promoted the application of CQDs [13,14]. For example, carboxylated CQDs can induce the synthesis of binary metal organic framework material (MOF) nanosheets, which is of great significance for the development of sustainable energy conversion and electrochemical technologies. Song et al. [15] adopted the simple room temperature “bottom-up” synthesis method to prepare 2D NiFe-MOF nanosheets in situ by applying CQDs-COOH. The results show that CQDs-COOH can increase the positive charge of the active site and improve the oxygen evolution reaction performance of NiFe-MOF. NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH shows a low overpotential of 261 mV and slight Tafel slope of 56 mV at the current density of 10 mA.cm−2. The common bottom-up CQD induced synthesis method provides a new solution for the efficient preparation of 2D MOF nanosheets. Additionally, electron-rich regions may be constructed by CQ, improving the electron conductivity of the electrode.

Inspired by these ideas, CQDs were synthesized in this paper by hydrothermal method and then introduced into NiCo-MOF to prepare CQDs/NiCo-MOF composites with nano thinner lamellar structure. The results showed that the specific capacitance of NiCo-MOF@CAn could reach 1917.7 F·g−1 at the potential scan rate of 5 mV·s−1, which was significantly higher than that of NiCo-MOF (1173.4 F·g−1).

All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade and did not require further purification before use. NiCl3·6H2O and CoCl3·6H2O (analytical grade) were purchased from Aladdin. Citric acid (analytical grade), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and terephthalic acid (PTA) were obtained from McLean. Ethylenediamine came from Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co. Potassium. Hydroxide (analytical grade) was from Beijing Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co.

The two-step process for the preparation of CQD-containing MOF composites NiCo-MOF@CA was as follows: (1) Hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the CQDs solution as shown in Fig. 1a. Citric acid (CA, 5 mmol) was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water, and then ethylenediamine (2 mL) was added to form a mixed solution. After being stirred for 10 min, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL stainless steel teflon reactor, which was immediately placed in a 160 °C oven for 4 h. After that, the obtained CQDs solution was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis bag of 1000 MW and evaporated to 20 mL for spare. (2) NiCo-MOF doped with CQDs was prepared as shown in Fig. 1b. Firstly, nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl3·6H2O, 1 mmol), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl3·6H2O, 1 mmol) and terephthalic acid (PTA, 2 mmol) were dispersed in N, N dimethylformamide (DMF, 30 ml) and stirred for 10 min. Next, the above prepared CQDs solution (CQD-CA, 10 mL) was added into the solution. After stirred for 5 min, the well-mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL teflon autoclave lined stainless steel, which was then kept in a 160 °C oven for 10 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the resulting sample was washed several times with ethanol and deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

In order to carry out a comparative study to explore the effect of CQDs on the structural properties of NiCo-MOF materials, NiCo-MOF without CQDs and nitrogen-doped CQDs/NiCo-MOF composite (which was denoted as NiCo-MOF@CAn) were additionally prepared. The preparation process was almost the same as that of NiCo-MOF@CA. For preparing NiCo-MOF, the 10 mL CQDs solution in the second step of preparing NiCo-MOF@CA was substituted with 10 mL deionized water. For preparing NiCo-MOF@CAn, in the first step, nitrogen-doped CQDs was synthesized by adding urea, and in the second step, 10 mL nitrogen-doped CQDs solution was used.

The crystal structures of the different materials were further analyzed by X-ray diffractometer. The diffraction peaks at 8.9°, 15.8° and 17.8° in Fig. 4 were the (100), (101) and (110) crystal planes of the Ni-MOF phase (CCDC 638866) [17]. The peak positions of the three materials were basically the same, indicating that the added CQDs did not affect the crystal phase structure of the material itself. As shown in the figure, the intensity of the diffraction peak at 17.8° had been enhanced to some extent, and the intensity of the diffraction peak at 14.2 had been weakened to a certain extent. It was speculated that the CQDs might modulate the ratio of Ni and Co in the composite to some degree.

In the FTIR spectrogram (Fig. 5a), all the prepared materials showed similar peaks, indicating that all the materials had almost the same function groups in the three samples. The broad peak at about 3400 cm−1 was the H2O molecule in the material. The peaks at 3426, 3422 cm−1 for NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn were NH bonds in urea. As for the CN bond synthesized from urea and citric acid, the peak intensity of NiCo-MOF@CAn at 1311 cm−1 was slightly stronger than that of NiCo-MOF@CA, implying their electron structure was some different.

By means of XPS, the elemental composition and valence state information of NiCo-MOF and NiCo-MOF@CAn were obtained. The full spectrum (Fig. 5b) showed that the composite material was mainly composed of elements C, O, Ni, and Co. The high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 5c–f) showed the detailed bonding information of C 1s, O1s, Ni 2p and Co 2p. The peaks appearing at 531.4, 531.9, and 533.1 eV in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) corresponded to O = M (Ni, Co), OH, and O=C-O chemical bonds, respectively. The peaks at 284.9, 285.9, and 288.6 eV in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) were assigned to the chemical bonds of C-C/C=C, CO, and CO. The atom C percentage content was 58.5 at.% in NiCo-MOF@CAn, slightly higher than in NiCo-MOF (56.8 at.%). This phenomenon might be attributed to the introduced CQDs, which had been observed as black nano-dots in the above TEM analysis. In the Ni 2p high-resolution spectrum in NiCo-MOF@CAn (Fig. 5e), the two main peaks locating at 856.7 and 874.5 eV corresponded to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively. In comparison with NiCo-MOF, these two peaks shifted by −0.2 and −0.3 eV, respectively. The weak peaks at 861.5 and 879.1 eV were satellite peaks. In Fig. 5f, the two weak satellite peaks located at 785.9 and 801.9 eV [18,19]. The peaks at 781.7 and 797.8 eV were ascribed to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 of NiCo-MOF@CAn, respectively. When compared with those peaks of NiCo-MOF, the two main peaks of Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2 in NiCo-MOF@CAn shifted by −0.1 and −0.34 eV, respectively. The above peak shifts demonstrated that the CQDs introduced by this method had a certain effect on the Ni and Co electronic structures of NiCo-MOF, and more electron transfer might be accepted. It was possible that the charge transfer from oxygen to Ni and Co occurred. As the result, there was some higher electron cloud density around active metals Ni and Co in NiCo-MOF@CAn than in NiCo-MOF. In addition, the amount of element N had changed. The N content in NiCo-MOF@CAn was 0.72 at. %, slightly higher than that in NiCo-MOF (0.52 at. %) [20].

The results of the nitrogen adsorption desorption experiment were shown in Fig. 6 . The adsorption/desorption isotherms of NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn almost overlapped, suggesting that the pore size distribution of these two materials was similar. It was worth noting that these two materials displayed higher specific surface and mesoporous structures than NiCo-MOF. Meanwhile, all the materials showed obvious H3 hysteresis loops, implying that the prepared samples had a typical mesoporous structure. In Fig. 6b, the pore size distribution curves of BJH confirmed their mesoporous structure, but the number of mesopores in NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn was significantly higher than that in NiCo-MOF. The pore size distribution was narrow and mainly concentrated in the range of 2–8 nm. The pore volumes of NiCo-MOF, NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn were 0.0352, 0.0603, and 0.0615 mL·g−1 respectively. Their corresponding BET surface areas were 5.34, 7.61, and 9.07 m2·g−1 respectively. The larger specific surface area and pore structure shown by NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn were in line with the results observed in SEM. The larger specific surface area could provide more electroactive centers for faradic reactions, which was beneficial for improving the specific capacitance of supercapacitors. Meanwhile, high porosity would promote the electrolyte ions to migrate rapidly, improving the capacity at high charging and discharging rate. Therefore, the composite materials of NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn were promising to show excellent electrochemical performance.

The preparation of electrode materials is divided into two steps, the first is the pretreatment of nickel foam, and the second is the coating of materials on nickel foam. The coating of MOF material on nickel foam was carried out as follows: the prepared MOF material sample, acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride were homogeneously mixed in a mortar and pestle in the ratio of 8:1:1, and then a small amount of N-methyl-2-pyridinyl ketone was added to form a homogeneous slurry. Finally, the slurry was applied on the nickel foam with a brush and kept in the oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The slurry was then mixed with a small amount of N-methyl-2-pyridyl alkanone.

The electrochemical properties of the samples were tested by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge discharge (GCD) and alternating current (AC) impedance methods in a three-electrode system, where 6 M KOH solution was used as the electrolyte. In the CV curves (Fig. 7a), all the samples showed distinct redox peaks in the range of 0–0.6 V at 5 mV·s−1, indicating that they had typical pseudocapacitive properties. The largest integration area of NiCo-MOF@CAn indicated that NiCo-MOF@CAn had the highest specific capacitance. The specific capacitances of NiCo-MOF, NiCo-MOF@CA, and NiCo-MOF@CAn at the scanning rate of 5 mV·s−1 were 1173.4, 1489.3 and 1917.7 F·g−1, respectively. The GCD curves (Fig. 7b) were in general agreement with the CV curves. They showed obvious charging and discharging plateau, also indicating that the materials were typical pseudocapacitive materials. In addition, GCD discharge curve of NiCo-MOF@CAn slowly decreased to the lower potential while that of NiCo-MOF decreased sharply. This phenomenon implied that NiCo-MOF@CAn possessed lower electrical resistivity than NiCo-MOF and then relatively slight polarization. From the above phenomena, it can be concluded that the introduced CQDs played an important role in NiCo-MOF's electrochemical performance.

Combined with the previous physical characterization, the reasons for the enhanced electrochemical performance were deduced as follows: (1) the morphology of NiCo-MOF was regulated by CQDs, increasing its specific surface area and pore size; (2) CQDs were able to regulate the ratio of nickel and cobalt metals in MOF, optimize its structure and the electronic conductivity, and make the electron transport more convenient; (3) CQDs increased the electron cloud density of nickel and cobalt ions, activated the metal center, and made it more convenient to transfer the electrons. (4) The higher electron conductivity of CQDs decreased charge transfer resistance of the composite electrode and then alleviated the polarization.

Fig. 8a presented the CV plots of NiCo-MOFs@CAn at 5–50 mV·s−1. The excellently symmetrical redox peaks indicated that NiCo-MOFs@CA had excellent reversibility performance. With the increase of the potential scanning rate, the oxidation and reduction peaks moved to positive and negative potentials, respectively, increasing the potential difference between the oxidation and reduction peaks. Meanwhile, the irreversible degree also increased at higher scan rates. This might be related with the electrochemical polarization.

In the GCD curves (Fig. 8b), the voltage plateaus matched with the redox peak potentials in the CV curves, further revealing the typical pseudocapacitive behaviour of NiCo-MOF@CAn composite electrode. Besides, the charging and discharging potential plateaus were shortened or even disappeared with the increase of current density. Obviously, the rate performance of NiCo-MOF-CAn was not ideal. It was concluded that the ion diffusion rate and electron transport rate were unable to keep pace with the reaction rate, causing the polarization phenomenon. Therefore, at higher current densities, the redox reaction occurred only in a small portion of the active sites, and the electrode material was not fully utilized.

Nyquist plots can directly reflect the electrochemical system resistance (Rs, the intercept on the high frequency axis), charge transfer resistance (Rct, the diameter of the semicircular arc), and the ion interfacial diffusion rate (the slope in the low frequency region). These specific values can be obtained from Nyquist plots. As could be seen in Fig. 9a, the values of Rs and Rct decreased with the introduction of CQDs, which further confirmed that the CQDs were favorable for enhancing the electrical conductivity of NiCo-MOF. The Rs and Rct values of NiCo-MOF@CAn were 0.85 and 0.82 Ω, respectively, 1.18 and 2.59 Ω for NiCo-MOF@CA. Both of them showed lower Rs and Rct than NiCo-MOF (Rs = 1.2 Ω and Rct = 2.94 Ω). Obviously, NiCo-MOF@CAn materials had excellent ion diffusion kinetics at the interface between electrolyte and electrode. This was related with its larger number of mesopores and higher porosity. As for their cycling performance, this synthesis method was not effective for improving the stability of NiCo-MOF. In Fig. 9b, after 1000 cycles at 15 A·g−1, the capacitance retention of NiCo-MOF@CA and NiCo-MOF@CAn was higher than 60 % (69.4 % and 65.2 % in reality), while that of NiCo-MOF was even lower than 60 %. As for the reason for the poor cycling performance, it mainly involved two aspects. On the one hand, the structural stability of MOF might collapse in the repeating charging and discharging; on the other hand, the CQDs might detach from NiCo-MOF material in the cycling, leading to an increase in electron resistance.

In summary, two different CQDs were successfully synthesized and hybridized with NiCo-MOF materials, leading to changes in morphology and structure, as well as an increase in specific surface area and pore size. The CQDs were able to regulate the ratio of nickel and cobalt in MOF, enhancing the electronic conductivity of the nickel‑cobalt metal by increasing its electronic cloud density of metal center nickel and cobalt. Besides the usual surfactants, other materials, such as CQDs with specific functional groups, are expected to modulate the morphology, porous structure and the partial electron structure of the target materials.

In electrochemical analysis, the NiCo-MOF@CAn material exhibited the best performance with a specific capacitance of 1917.7 F·g−1 at the scan rate of 5 mV·s−1, and the NiCo-MOF@CA material also showed improved specific capacitance of 1489.3 F·g−1 compared to NiCo-MOF. The hybridized NiCo-MOF material with CQDs showed lower Rs and Rct values due to faster electron transfer in the electrolyte. However, the cyclic performance was not ideal although some improvement was achieved some improvement after doping. Therefore, further optimization is needed to enhance the cyclic performance in future work.

Guoping Wang: Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tian Tang: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Lilei Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sen Liao: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (grant number 2019JJ60031), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (grant number 2016JJ3105). The Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China [2018JJ3447].

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nickel-based metal-organic frameworks as electrocatalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER).
